EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level ridge over the Desert Southwest led to continued above normal temperatures along with excessive heat conditions for the middle part of September. Temperatures most afternoons during this time in Phoenix topped out around 108-109 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 10 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time. One of these fatalities included a person who died while hiking in Scottsdale. According to local media reports, a couple from Rhode Island started hiking around noon on the 12th at Brown's Ranch Trailhead in Scottsdale. After splitting up during the hike, the wife never returned to their meeting location. Local authorities responded and discovered that the wife had succumbed to the heat.

Deep monsoon moisture over northern Arizona and an area of low pressure moving over the area from the east brought more thunderstorms with heavy rain, flooding, and at least one funnel cloud. A mesoscale convective complex formed late in the day and kept thunderstorms going through the night and into the morning of July 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters swept a vehicle away in a low water crossing on Camino Real near Rocky Knolls Road. The flood water was estimated to be 8 feet deep. The body of the driver was found several days later at the confluence with the Verde River.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptional (D4) and extreme (D3) drought categories continued across a large part of southeast Arizona during January, with the exception of western Pima County. Precipitation totals for the month totaled 0.5 to 1.5 inches, with higher amounts in the form of snow on the highest mountains. These totals were 25 to 125 percent of normal, but even where substantial precipitation occurred, were not enough to improve the drought status. Soil moisture readings improved slightly, but were still below the 5th percentile ranking across Santa Cruz and eastern sections of Pima and Pinal counties and below the 10th percentile ranking elsewhere. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir remained around 2 percent of storage capacity.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the southern slopes of Mt Elden caused flash flooding in northeast Flagstaff.  The water overwhelmed drainage systems and was flowing rapidly down streets (up to two feet deep). A video on social media showed a Prius floating down the street and crashing into a full sized pick-up truck.  Water was flowing 6 feet deep in a low water crossing.  Famous Route 66 had 2 feet of water flowing over it in town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood rushed down the Tatahatso Wash/Slot Canyon into the Colorado River (38 miles down stream from Lees Ferry). A commercial river trip with 30 people and two large raft had set up camp at the mouth of the wash for the night. The flash flood washed away the camp. Four people were injured and one person died.

These totals were 25 to 125 percent of normal, but even where substantial precipitation occurred, were not enough to improve the drought status. Soil moisture readings improved slightly, but were still below the 5th percentile ranking across Santa Cruz and eastern sections of Pima and Pinal counties and below the 10th percentile ranking elsewhere. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir remained around 2 percent of storage capacity.

Very hot temperatures were experienced west and south of Tucson September 11 through 14. The hottest two days were the 12th and 13th when high temperatures fell just shy of records across much of Pima County and topped out between 104 and 109 degrees. During the four day period, the Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for seven individuals found in the desert, one at Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument, five on the Tohono O'odham Nation, and one in Santa Cruz County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 3 to 5 degrees above normal, around 105 degrees, were experienced across the western and central Pima County causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached on September 14. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two males found in the desert, one on the eastern edge of the Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument and another on the Tohono O'odham Nation northwest of Kohatk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of winter storm systems moved across Arizona January 24 through 26. The first brought moderate snow accumulations to several mountain ranges above 6000 feet on January 24. Mt. Lemmon Highway was closed at the base as a result. Thunderstorms produced hail and gusty winds at lower elevations. The second storm brought widespread heavy snow to southeast Arizona above 3500 feet, and even brought slushy  snow accumulations and slippery road conditions to parts of the greater Tucson Metro area. The bulk of the snow fell from the evening of January 25 through the morning of January 26, and was accompanied at times by wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches occurred between 3500 and 5000 feet with 6 to 12 inches from 5000 to 7000 feet and 12 to 20 inches above 7000 feet. The additional snow forced the closure of Mt. Lemmon Highway near Tucson to continue, and also triggered closures of Highway 191 north of Hannagan Meadow and Route 80 near Bisbee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations of 16 to 20 inches combined with winds gusting to 30 mph caused blowing and drifting snow on Mt. Graham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lightning caused Walnut Fire started June 20 in the Little Dragoon Mountains in Cochise County. The wildfire grew rapidly to nearly 7000 acres in hot, dry and occasionally windy conditions by June 22, jumping Interstate 10 and prompting its closure and the evacuation of several residences and businesses in the Texas Canyon and Dragoon areas. Rain on the 23rd helped slow the fire's spread and additional rain on the 28th quashed the fire after it consumed a total of 10,667 acres. Fire suppression costs tallied $1.4 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human caused Pinnacle Fire started June 10 on Pinnacle Ridge in the Santa Teresa Wilderness of western Graham County. The fire grew to over 15,000 acres within a week in hot, dry and windy conditions and to 34,417 acres by June 25. No fire growth was experienced after June 25, but the fire was not officially contained until July 16. Residents of the Aravaipa, Black Rock and Klondyke communities were evacuated for about two weeks, but no structures were lost. Fire suppression costs tallied $10.5 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human caused Salt Fire started on April 8th on San Carlos Apache Tribe lands near Bylas in northwest Graham County. On the first day, the fire burned over 2000 acres in the Gila River bed having been spread by winds gusting to more than 30 mph. Two homes were evacuated in Bylas as a precaution, but the only structures damaged were two abandoned corrals. The fire was contained April 14th, but not before it consumed 4425 acres and came within a mile of Fort Thomas. Fire suppression costs tallied $350,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bonito Rock Fire burned through 1,688 acres by the end of April in an area 15 miles southeast of Whiteriver, Arizona.  At least 173 personnel were involved with the fire fighting effort. The fuels involved	 were primarily timber (litter and understory), timber (grass and understory), and brush (2 feet).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bonito Rock burned through approximately 1,688 acres in southern Apache County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The G22 Fire burned through at least 2,020 acres in southern Navajo County around 18 miles south-southwest of Heber, Arizona. At least 56 people were involved in the fire fighting effort. The fire was in timber (litter and understory), brush (2 feet), and  medium logging slash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The G22 Fire burned through at least 2,020 acres 18 miles south-southeast of Heber, AZ.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human caused Jack Fire started on April 21st in the Chiricahua Mountains of southeast Cochise County.  The wildfire was driven by wind gusts to 40 mph at times and over 1600 acres were burned in the first 24 hours.  Forward progress was stopped on April 25th and the fire was contained April 28th with a total of 1856 acres consumed. Fire suppression costs tallied just over $2 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure centered over the Great Basin and an inverted trough near the Arizona/Mexico international border promoted enhanced east/northeast steering flow favorable for maintaining thunderstorms off the high terrain into the lower elevations. A very moist and unstable atmosphere promoted the development of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed off the high terrain of southern Gila County and into the lower deserts during the evening of the 15th. Several instances of damage were reported, which included multiple downed trees in the Mesa area. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging wind gusts moving through the Roosevelt Lake area resulted in a metal carport roof being blown off. Wind gusts estimated to be between 50-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

Several instances of damage were reported, which included multiple downed trees in the Mesa area. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large pine tree approximately 2-3 feet in diameter fell on top of a garage and a vehicle near University Drive and Greenfield Road in Mesa. No injuries were reported. Timing was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon of the 28th, scattered showers passed over the greater Phoenix metro area. Due to the dry sub-cloud environment, a cluster of stronger showers over the eastern metro produced a strong outflow boundary that moved west through the city with wind gusts measured around 40-45 mph, including a 46 mph gust at Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport. The outflow also produced blowing dust on the southwest side of the city with a couple reports of visibility down to around 1 mile. Just ahead of the westward-moving outflow, a weak shower produced a downburst which generated dense blowing dust, less than a quarter mile visibility, along Interstate-10 near Buckeye Municipal Airport. A Significant Weather Advisory was issued for the outflow winds and a Dust Advisory for the blowing dust. No significant traffic interruptions, damages, or injuries were report with the blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility fell to less than a quarter mile in blowing dust along Interstate-10 near exit 109 to Palo Verde road. The report was supported by a dashcam photo from the truck driver on I-10 that submitted the report. There were no reports of significant traffic disruptions and no reports of damages nor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptional (D4) and extreme (D3) drought categories continued across a large part of southeast Arizona during April, with the exception of western Pima County. |Precipitation totals for the month totaled less than 0.25 inch, or less than 25 percent of normal, in most areas, but a few locations from the Mogollon Rim across western Graham/eastern Pinal counties into east central Pima County received between 0.25 and 0.75 inch of rain. Soil moisture readings remained very low, below the 5th percentile ranking across Santa Cruz, eastern Pima and Pinal and western Graham counties and below the 10th percentile ranking elsewhere. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir fell close to zero percent of capacity with only 50 acre-feet--the lowest level in more than a decade. At least a dozen small wild land fires occurred during the month, three of which  grew to over 1000 acres due to dry conditions and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The exceptional (D4) and extreme (D3) drought categories continued across a large part of southeast Arizona during April, with the exception of western Pima County. |Precipitation totals for the month totaled less than 0.25 inch, or less than 25 percent of normal, in most areas, but a few locations from the Mogollon Rim across western Graham/eastern Pinal counties into east central Pima County received between 0.25 and 0.75 inch of rain. Soil moisture readings remained very low, below the 5th percentile ranking across Santa Cruz, eastern Pima and Pinal and western Graham counties and below the 10th percentile ranking elsewhere. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir fell close to zero percent of capacity with only 50 acre-feet--the lowest level in more than a decade. At least a dozen small wild land fires occurred during the month, three of which  grew to over 1000 acres due to dry conditions and gusty winds.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptional (D4) and extreme (D3) drought categories continued across a large part of southeast Arizona during February, with the exception of western Pima County. Precipitation totals for the month totaled 0.5 to 2.0 inches near the Mogollon Rim or 50 to 100 percent of normal, but less than a half inch elsewhere, or zero to 50 percent of normal.  Soil moisture readings remained very low, below the 5th percentile ranking across eastern Pinal and western Graham counties and below the 10th percentile ranking elsewhere. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir remained around 2 percent of storage capacity. More than a dozen small wild land fires occurred during the month, but all were quickly suppressed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, nearly stationary high pressure led to a week long, intense heat wave with many temperature records broken.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat Warning criteria were reached seven days in a row.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure centered over the Great Basin and an inverted trough near the Arizona/Mexico international border promoted enhanced east/northeast steering flow favorable for maintaining thunderstorms off the high terrain into the lower elevations. A very moist and unstable atmosphere promoted the development of strong to severe thunderstorms that pushed off the high terrain of southern Gila County and into the lower deserts during the evening of the 15th. Several instances of damage were reported, which included multiple downed trees in the Mesa area. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos on social media showed several large trees down along with damage to a playground in an area near Arizona Avenue between Elliot Road and Warner Road. A large tree estimated to be approximately 2-3 feet in diameter was uprooted at an apartment complex at Warner Road and Evergreen Street. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of winter storm systems moved across Arizona January 24 through 26. The first brought moderate snow accumulations to several mountain ranges above 6000 feet on January 24. Mt. Lemmon Highway was closed at the base as a result. Thunderstorms produced hail and gusty winds at lower elevations. The second storm brought widespread heavy snow to southeast Arizona above 3500 feet, and even brought slushy  snow accumulations and slippery road conditions to parts of the greater Tucson Metro area. The bulk of the snow fell from the evening of January 25 through the morning of January 26, and was accompanied at times by wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches occurred between 3500 and 5000 feet with 6 to 12 inches from 5000 to 7000 feet and 12 to 20 inches above 7000 feet. The additional snow forced the closure of Mt. Lemmon Highway near Tucson to continue, and also triggered closures of Highway 191 north of Hannagan Meadow and Route 80 near Bisbee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations of 10 to 14 inches combined with winds gusting to 40 mph occurred in the Chiricahua Mountains.  Eleven inches of snow was observed at an elevation of 5500 feet at Paradise.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations of 10 to 14 inches combined with winds gusting to 40 mph caused blowing and drifting snow in the Santa Catalina and Rincon Mountains.  Mount Lemmon Highway remained closed at the base due to the heavy snow.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of winter storm systems moved across Arizona January 24 through 26. The first brought moderate snow accumulations to several mountain ranges above 6000 feet on January 24. Mt. Lemmon Highway was closed at the base as a result. Thunderstorms produced hail and gusty winds at lower elevations. The bulk of the snow fell from the evening of January 25 through the morning of January 26, and was accompanied at times by wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches occurred between 3500 and 5000 feet with 6 to 12 inches from 5000 to 7000 feet and 12 to 20 inches above 7000 feet. The additional snow forced the closure of Mt. Lemmon Highway near Tucson to continue, and also triggered closures of Highway 191 north of Hannagan Meadow and Route 80 near Bisbee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and snow showers created slippery roads in parts of eastern Pima County, which led to numerous, mainly minor, traffic accidents along Interstate 19 near Green Valley and Sahuarita and along Interstate 10 in the northwest Tucson Metro area.  One accident on I-10 near the Cochise County line resulted in a rollover, which caused one east bound lane to be closed for an hour. No injuries were reported.

The second storm brought widespread heavy snow to southeast Arizona above 3500 feet, and even brought slushy  snow accumulations and slippery road conditions to parts of the greater Tucson Metro area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused minor roof damage to a home in North Komelik.  Rain caused further damage inside the home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations of 10 to 14 inches combined with winds gusting to 50 mph caused blowing and drifting snow in the Huachuca, Santa Rita and Mule mountains. Ten inches of snow was observed at an elevation of 5500 feet in Bisbee. State Route 80 near Bisbee was closed due to the snow and Cochise County closed most of its government offices on January 26. After the snow ended, a hiker who became stranded during the winter storm was rescued by helicopter from the Huachuca Mountains.  The hiker was treated for frostbite by medical staff.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN COCONINO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds caused blowing dust that hit both Winslow and Tuba City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dust storm moved from north to south across the Little Colorado River Valley near Winslow with visibility down to between 1/4 to 1/2 miles. Significant blowing dust was also observed in Tuba City.  The winds were gusting to near 40 MPH around this event.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds caused blowing dust that hit both Winslow and Tuba City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dust from a thunderstorm gust front dropped the visibility at the Winslow Airport down to 4 miles at the Winslow airport with gusts to 36 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture moving northward into Arizona brought thunderstorms and gusty winds to the Verde Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montezuma Airpark (Site ID  KAZcampV9) reported a peak wind gust of 58 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rafael Fire was started by lightning June 18 and grew to just over 78,000 acres by the end of June. Little if any growth was observed in July before the rainy season started.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rafael Fire was started by lightning June 18 and grew to just over 78,000 acres by the end of June. Little if any growth was observed in July before the rainy season started. The fire was located 4 miles north of Perkinsville, Arizona and east of Highway 89 in The Prescott National Forest.  There were three other lightning caused fires in the area: Sand Flat, Archibald, and Horney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tiger fire was started by lightning and grew to about 16,300 acres before it was contained by the end of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tiger Fire was started by lightning eleven miles east of the town of Crown King in short grass, brush, and timber.  The fire was relatively small by the end of June. However, it grew to over 12,000 acres in the first week of July.  the final size was around 16,300 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human caused Margo Fire started on April 8th in the San Pedro River bed near Dudleyville. The fire was spread by gusty winds which led to the evacuation of over 200 of the town's residents.  The wildfire destroyed seventeen residences and around two dozen outbuildings and caused the closure of State Route 77. The fire consumed 1148 acres before it was contained April 11th. Property damage costs were estimated at around $2 million while fire suppression costs tallied $300,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first heat wave of the season pushed temperatures well over 100 degrees in much of the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat Warning criteria were reached four days in a row.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, nearly stationary high pressure led to a week long, intense heat wave with many temperature records broken.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat Warning criteria were reached seven days in a row.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptional (D4) and extreme (D3) drought categories continued across a large part of southeast Arizona during April, with the exception of western Pima County. |Precipitation totals for the month totaled less than 0.25 inch, or less than 25 percent of normal, in most areas, but a few locations from the Mogollon Rim across western Graham/eastern Pinal counties into east central Pima County received between 0.25 and 0.75 inch of rain. Soil moisture readings remained very low, below the 5th percentile ranking across Santa Cruz, eastern Pima and Pinal and western Graham counties and below the 10th percentile ranking elsewhere. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir fell close to zero percent of capacity with only 50 acre-feet--the lowest level in more than a decade. At least a dozen small wild land fires occurred during the month, three of which  grew to over 1000 acres due to dry conditions and gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system led to very strong gradient winds across south-central Arizona on the 3rd. Southerly to southwesterly wind gusts were measured upwards of 45-55 mph from late morning through the afternoon. The winds produced widespread blowing dust in Pinal and eastern Maricopa counties. The winds and dust created difficult to dangerous travel conditions, especially along I-10, and a residence in Apache Junction saw minor roof damage. A wind advisory and several blowing dust advisories were issued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts up to 50-55 mph caused minor damage to the roof of a residence in Apache Junction near Broadway Rd and Ironwood Rd. A trained spotter reported asphalt roof tiles in a 4 foot by 5 foot area were blown off. No other damage was reported and there were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds created areas of blowing dust that reduced visibility to less than a quarter mile and resulted in a multi-vehicle accident and closure of Interstate 10 in Pinal County.  Several injuries to motorists occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds created areas of blowing dust in south central Pinal County. Visibility was reduced to less than a quarter mile along Interstate 10 near Picacho and resulted in a multi-vehicle accident and a closure of the east bound lanes of the interstate for 60-90 minutes. Thirteen motorists were injured. Five people were taken to the hospital but none of the injuries were life threatening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system brought gusty south winds to the Mojave Desert, with isolated high winds observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak measured gust occurred at the Kingman Airport (KIGM). A power line was also blown down in the city of Kingman.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure moving south off  the West Coast and a short wave rotating around this low brought strong southwest to parts of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Springerville Airport reported sustained wind speeds of 40 MPH or higher between 215 PM and 455 PM.  The peak wind gust of 59 MPH was recorded at 315 PM MST.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure moving south off  the West Coast and a short wave rotating around this low brought strong southwest to parts of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ADOT wind sensor at 14 Mile Hill on Highway 377 reported a peak wind gust of 58 MPH at 433 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low helped fuel a mix of showers and virga over the Mojave Desert. One virga shaft produced a microburst which caused minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two carports in Lake Havasu City were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving large area of low pressure brought precipitation over northern Arizona for several days.  Some area received heavy amounts of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slide Rock State Park (just below 5000 feet) reported 4 inches of snow by 330 PM on March 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving large area of low pressure brought precipitation over northern Arizona for several days.  Some area received heavy amounts of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two 2 inches fell overnight Friday into Saturday (March 12-13) and four 4 inches additional fell on Saturday evening.  The elevation was just below 4,800 feet.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN COCONINO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold trough moved southward through Nevada and closed off over Arizona as it shifted eastward into New Mexico. This produces showery weather over much of northern Arizona with a band of heavier snow just ahead of the low.  The heavier precipitation fell along a line from Williams to Window Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow fell 5 miles north of Angell at an elevation of around 5,600 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm and its associated cold front rolled through the central Arizona deserts on January 25th, leading to widespread wind damage across much of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. A line of strong to severe thunderstorms associated with the front generated wind gusts in excess of 60 mph in some locations and the gusty severe winds downed numerous trees, caused damage to roofs and fencing, and in southwest Mesa managed to blow a rooftop air conditioner off of the roof. More than 30,000 utility customers across the Valley lost power at the height of the storm as numerous power poles were snapped or blown over. Several Phoenix area outdoor COVID-19 vaccination sites saw some minor damage to the tents and overall setup from the strong winds, which led to some delays. Rain cooled air associated with the strong storms lowered afternoon temperatures at Phoenix Sky Harbor airport from around 50 degrees to 36 degrees in 25 minutes. Many areas saw pea-sized hail as the storms moved through the Valley. There were no reports of injuries associated with the damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with a strong winter storm moved through the central portions of the greater Phoenix area during the early afternoon hours on January 25th. A line of severe thunderstorms developed as the front moved through and they generated gusty and damaging outflow winds. According to a report from the public, at 1355MST gusty winds estimated at 65 mph knocked down two large trees into an apartment complex at 15th Avenue and Camelback Road, just to the east of Interstate 17, causing major structural damage to the apartment and minor damage to a car. At roughly the same time and a few miles to the east, local broadcast media reported that similarly strong outflow winds caused a palm tree to snap at its base and the tree fell onto an apartment complex located near 12th Street and Highland Avenue. The falling palm tree crashed through a second story unit with the single resident and his dog present at the time. Fortunately there were no reports of injuries at either location due to the falling trees. An COVID-19 vaccination site located at the State Fairgrounds sustained minor damage as the strong winds moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of tropical moisture, combined with a an upper level low centered over an area near state borders of Arizona, Nevada and California is producing moderate rainfall over|central and northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Verde River near Childs (Gauge EVDA3) rose from 10 to 12.4 feet in less than 30 minutes at 930 AM on September 1.  It dropped below 12 feet about 3 hours later. At 830 PM,  it rose to 13.8 feet.  The water dropped back to around 11.6 feet by 900 AM on September 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of tropical moisture, combined with a an upper level low centered over an area near state borders of Arizona, Nevada and California is producing moderate rainfall over|central and northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Little Colorado River Basin and tributaries created a surge of water at the river gauge (LCCA3) on the Little Colorado River near Cameron.  The water rose from 1.8 feet to 5.43 feet on the gauge in less than 15 minutes at 1245 AM on 2 September.  The water was still above 4.7 feet at 945 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of tropical moisture, combined with a an upper level low centered over an area near state borders of Arizona, Nevada and California is producing moderate rainfall over|central and northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gauge on Moenkopi Wash near Tuba City rose from 17.6 feet to 20.8 feet in less than half an hour at 430 PM on 1 September. The gauge went down to 18.4 feet then rose again to 23.7 feet in less than half an hour at 900 PM on 1 September.  The water rose a third time to 22 feet in less than half a hour at 3:00 AM on 2 September. The water receded below 18 feet by 800 AM on 2 September.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with a strong winter storm pushed east and across the western portion of the greater Phoenix area during early afternoon hours on January 25th. A line of strong to severe thunderstorms associated with the front generated some gusty and damaging outflow winds. At 1325MST a public social media post showed a large Palo Verde tree approximately one foot in diameter that was blown down and crashed through a concrete wall and onto a house. Another social media post showed the roof of a gazebo ripped off and a concrete block wall blown over. Winds were estimated to be at least 55 mph in strength. According to reports from local broadcast media, at 1336MST gusty winds estimated up to 60 mph ripped the roof off a home and also ripped off roof singles from a few other homes in Sun City; the winds also downed a couple of Palo Verde trees and blew down some fencing. The damage occurred about 1 mile northeast of Youngtown, and along 107th Avenue just to the south of Alabama Avenue. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold weather system impacted the Desert Southwest during late January. The storm produced significant snowfall for elevations above 3,500-4,000 feet over the course of the two days, mainly north and east of the Phoenix area. This led to the closures of US-60 between Globe and Show Low and SR-87 between Bush Highway and Payson. Following the passage of a cold front and squall line through south-central AZ, midday on the 25th, snow levels briefly crashed to as low as 1500-2000 feet. This resulted in light snow/graupel accumulations at these lower elevations which included the Wickenburg area and foothill regions just north and east of the Phoenix metro area. Mixed precipitation (rain, some snowflakes, graupel, and small hail) was also reported across the Phoenix metro, with some accumulating small hail or graupel. This created a large media interest across the Phoenix area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold weather system moved through the region and produced multiple rounds of snowfall along the Mazatzal mountains and portions of SR-87, mostly from midday of the 25th through the morning of the 26th. A Winter Storm Warning was in effect during this time for elevations above 4,000 feet. The heaviest snow likely fell in the afternoon of the 25th when a squall line/cold front moved west to east through the area, producing greater precipitation rates and lowering snow levels briefly as low as 1,500-2,000 feet. No official snowfall measurements were reported, but the Arizona Department of Transportation (ADOT) shared multiple images from SR-87 near Mt. Ord in the afternoon of the 26th showing several inches of snow had accumulated. ADOT had closed SR-87 between Bush Highway and Payson beginning at around 1600 MST on the 25th and eventually reopened the highway around 1900 MST on the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure situated over the Desert Southwest led to excessive heat conditions across the Phoenix area as temperatures climbed near 110 degrees. In Phoenix on the 12th, the afternoon high temperature fell one degree short of tying the daily record of 110 degrees. According to local media reports, a couple from Rhode Island started hiking around noon on the 12th at Brown's Ranch Trailhead in Scottsdale. After splitting up during the hike, the wife never returned to their meeting location. Local authorities responded and discovered that the wife had succumbed to the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple from Rhode Island started hiking around noon on the 12th at Brown's Ranch Trailhead in Scottsdale. After splitting up during the hike, the wife never returned to their meeting location. Local authorities from various agencies responded to search for the missing woman. Shortly after 2100MST, local authorities found the missing woman who had succumbed to the heat.

A trained spotter in Miami, at roughly 3,500 feet, initially reported snow was falling with less than a quarter mile visibility at 1600 MST on the 25th, but that snow had not begun to accumulate yet. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm and its associated cold front rolled through the central Arizona deserts on January 25th, leading to widespread wind damage across much of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. A line of strong to severe thunderstorms associated with the front generated wind gusts in excess of 60 mph in some locations and the gusty severe winds downed numerous trees, caused damage to roofs and fencing, and in southwest Mesa managed to blow a rooftop air conditioner off of the roof. More than 30,000 utility customers across the Valley lost power at the height of the storm as numerous power poles were snapped or blown over. Several Phoenix area outdoor COVID-19 vaccination sites saw some minor damage to the tents and overall setup from the strong winds, which led to some delays. Rain cooled air associated with the strong storms lowered afternoon temperatures at Phoenix Sky Harbor airport from around 50 degrees to 36 degrees in 25 minutes. Many areas saw pea-sized hail as the storms moved through the Valley. There were no reports of injuries associated with the damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with a strong winter storm moved through the southeast portion of the greater Phoenix area during the early afternoon on January 25th. A line of strong to severe thunderstorms associated with the front produced strong wind gusts estimated to be in excess of 60 mph at times. According to a trained weather spotter located 5 miles southwest of Mesa, the strong winds blew a rooftop air conditioner off of the roof at about 1345MST. Additionally, roof shingles were stripped off of rooftops in the area as the winds blew through the area. The damage occurred southeast of the intersection of Baseline and Cooper roads. There were no reports of injury due to the damaging winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jacks Canyon Creek near Winslow rose 2.8 feet in 90 minutes from 2.2 feet to 5 feet at 945 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough stretched from Utah southward into Arizona and a cold front near the Utah-Arizona border gradually moved southward triggering strong thunderstorms over far northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produce hail up to 1 and 3/4 inches in diameter about 9 miles west-southwest of Jacobs Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Boggy Creek Fire was started by lightning on May 23 in the Wallow Fire (2011 -538,000 acres) scar in steep rugged terrain in an area with heavy dead and down snags. The fire was 15 Miles SW of Alpine, Arizona and burned in timber (litter and understory) and short grass (1 foot).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Boggy Creek Fire burned through at least 3,000 acres by the end of May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The McDonald Take Fire started on May 23 and burned through 3,500 acres by the end of May 25 miles southeast of Whiteriver, Arizona.  The fire was south of Tonto Lake near the Black River on the Fort Apache Indian Nation.  The fuel types involved include: timber (litter and understory), brush (2 feet), tall grass (2.5 feet). The fuels were dry across all types and classes. Live fuel moistures are at critical levels and provided no suppressing effect on fire behavior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The McDonald Fire burned through 3,500 acres in southern Apache County by the end of May.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN COCONINO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, broad area of low pressure brought strong and gusty southwest winds to northern Arizona. The base of the trough rotating through the area during the afternoon helped to increase the pressure gradient especially across eastern Arizona. Most of the highest wind speeds were between 50 and 60 MPH with a few were higher than 60 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Winslow Airport (just one mile from the Coconino County Line) had a peak wind gust of 59 MPH at 203 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tussock Fire burned 5,546 acres about 7 miles southwest of Crown King.  The human caused fire burned across areas that are above/below 5000 feet (Yavapai County Mountains/Yavapai County Valleys and Basins).  The fire burned through short Grass (1 foot), brush (2 feet), and chaparral (6 feet).  The fire was primarily been carried by short brush and sparse grasses in the lower elevations and dense cured brush in the mid-elevation drainages. ||GR1; short sparse grass and cacti (hairy grama, blue grama, cacti). GS2; cured, grama/muttongrass and scattered short shrubs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tussock Fire burned across portions of the Yavapai County Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold trough moved southward through Nevada and closed off over Arizona as it shifted eastward into New Mexico. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagstaff area received 3 to 9 inches of snow. Other areas include (in inches):  13 miles NNW Flagstaff (8,200 feet) 9.4, Williams 4-8, and Doney Park 3-8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture increasing from the south brought thunderstorms across northern Arizona. The outflow boundary form one of the thunderstorms produced a landspout tornado over Doney Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported a brief weak spin-up of a landspout tornado in Doney Park. A gust from from a thunderstorm was moving through the area at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture increasing from the south brought thunderstorms across northern Arizona and southern Utah. Heavy Rain in Utah produced flash flooding in the Paria River that flows into Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture increasing from the south brought thunderstorms across northern Arizona. Heavy Rain in Utah produced flash flooding in the Paria River that flows into Arizona and the gauge near Lees Ferry rose from 4.4 feet to 6.3 feet at 4:30 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient developed across the Desert Southwest with a passing trough on the 9th. This gradient produced strong surface wind gusts up to 35-45 mph, which was strong enough to create patchy blowing dust and even a dense channel of blowing dust in northwest Pinal county in the afternoon. The channel of dust blew across Highway 347 north of Maricopa and lowered visibility to 0.25 miles or less. No accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 5 PM MST, a dust channel was noticed on satellite. At roughly 5:20 PM MST, an Arizona Department of Transportation (ADOT) webcam showed visibility within this dust channel had fallen to or below a quarter of a mile along Highway 347 between the town of Maricopa and the greater Phoenix area. A dust storm warning was issued in response. Visibility improved on the webcam at around 6:10 PM MST. Wind gusts in the area were observed up to 40-45 mph. No traffic incidents were reported on Highway 347 due to the dust and winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving large area of low pressure brought precipitation over northern Arizona for several days.  Some area received heavy amounts of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall over the Western Mogollon Rim late in the afternoon on March 11th. The snow continued to fall through the morning of March 14th.  The heaviest snow fell during the first night of the event. Heavy snow criteria was met before noon on March 12th for many locations across the Western Mogollon Rim. Snow then continued to fall at lighter intensities into the early morning hours of March 14 before ending.  Some of the snowfall totals include (in inches): Flagstaff and Kachina Village 12-17, Belelmont 11.5,  5WSW Parks 16.5, and  Parks 11.8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptional (D4) and extreme (D3) drought categories continued across a large part of southeast Arizona during May, with the exception of western Pima County. Rain for the month totaled less than 0.10 inch in most areas with spotty amounts of 0.25 to 0.50 inch on May 20-21. Showers on May 14 only produced light rain, but gusty winds produced blowing dust from Willcox to San Simon. Soil moisture readings remained below the 5th percentile ranking, except around the 10th percentile ranking in Greenlee and southeast Graham counties. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir remained near zero percent of capacity.  At least two dozen small wild land fires occurred during the month, one of which, the Zacattack fire, caused the evacuation of 30 homes in Old Bisbee's Brewery Gulch, but no damage.  Three wildfires--Sycamore Canyon, Warren and Warsaw--grew to around or over 1000 acres.

No fire growth was experienced after this date but the fire was not officially contained until June 4.  Fire suppression costs tallied over $3.5 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet July caused the exceptional (D4) drought category and extreme (D3) drought category to cease across nearly all of southeast Arizona, with exceptional drought only remaining in far southeast Cochise County by the end of the month. The extreme (D3) drought category remained across the rest of eastern and central Cochise County, much of Graham and Greenlee counties and parts of the Tohono O'odham Nation. July rainfall varied widely from 2 to 15 inches across southeast Arizona. In areas where the drought lingered through the end of the month this equated to 75 to 125 percent of normal rainfall while areas in which the drought eased further rainfall tallied as much as 400 percent of normal. July ranked as the wettest on record for any month at the Tucson International Airport where 8.06 inches was recorded. The rainfall caused wildfire season to end and soil moisture readings to improve from below the 10th percentile to between the 30th and 70th percentiles by month's end. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir started to rise, but only reached slightly more than 1 percent of capacity by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture and clearing skies lead to the development of thunderstorms that produced heavy rain and flash flooding over Yavapai County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge on Granite Creek (northeast of downtown Prescott--GRCA3) reported a 6.7 foot rise with a peak flow of 3,558 CFS.  At least five roads were closed in Prescott due to flooding between 2:50 and 3:50 PM.  Several roads were also closed in Wilhoit due to flooding.  Two cars were stuck in flowing washes near Chino Valley/Paulden. Water was beginning to move the vehicles so Law Enforcement was called to the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture over northern Arizona allowed thunderstorms to develop over central Arizona. Some thunderstorms produced damaging winds during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Humbug Creek RAWS(QHBA3) had a peak wind gust of 67 MPH at 9:40 PM.  The  Stanton RAWS (QSTA3) had 60 MPH winds at 10:00 PM. Arizona Department of Transportation reported that State Route 89 south of Prescott (mile marker 308) was closed in both directions due to fallen trees at 11:35 PM.  The exact time of tree fall was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture over northern Arizona lead to flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Museum Fire scar (2019) produce significant flooding down Spruce Wash and into north central Flagstaff and down into the Rio de Flag on the south and southeast Flagstaff. The flood water first entered developed area around 1245 PM with trees, logs, and other debris 60-70 feet wide over topping a road about 2.5 - 3 feet deep. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures were experienced in much of southeast Arizona from June 12-20 with high temperatures exceeding established records in many locations. A number of  heat-related deaths were reported of persons found in desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot high temperatures of 106 to 119 degrees, or 8 to 13 degrees above normal, occurred in the lower elevations of southeast Arizona June 12-20, and caused the High Heat Risk category to be reached. New record daily high temperature records were established at numerous sites on several days during this period. For Tucson International Airport, this nine day period contributed to the hottest June on record and the 4th hottest temperature on record of 115 degrees occurred on June 15.  Sites that tied or broke all time hottest temperature records included Ajo with 119 degrees, Sasabe with 113, Green Valley with 112. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for 14 people found in the desert of Pima and Santa Cruz counties during this time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following widespread 1 to 2 inches of rain on Christmas Eve, dense fog developed in parts of the Greater Phoenix Area in the morning of Christmas Day, particularly in the southwest and far southeast parts of the metro. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure from southern California through southern Nevada helped  draw monsoon moisture northward across Arizona. Scattered showers and storms  developed over the high terrain. A few  of the storms produced rain rates of nearly 2 inches an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Verde River near Camp Verde rose almost two feet with a crest of 4.39 feet at 6:00 PM MST (VAVA3).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the region transitioned out of the monsoon, a compact low created a high shear - modest CAPE environment. Parameters were sufficient for supercell development and a few supercells did develop amongst isolated to scattered storms in the afternoon across South-central Arizona, including a right-mover that pushed into Phoenix and a left split. The environment was supportive of severe to large hail with lower freezing levels and steep mid-level lapse rates. Measured hail stones up to 1.0 to 1.5 diameter was observed with the few supercells. Most other storms also produced smaller hail throughout the day. Damaging winds and isolated flash flooding was also observed through the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media photo of estimated nickel-sized hail.

Parameters were sufficient for supercell development and a few supercells did develop amongst isolated to scattered storms in the afternoon across South-central Arizona, including a right-mover that pushed into Phoenix and a left split. The environment was supportive of severe to large hail with lower freezing levels and steep mid-level lapse rates. Measured hail stones up to 1.0 to 1.5 diameter was observed with the few supercells. Most other storms also produced smaller hail throughout the day. Damaging winds and isolated flash flooding was also observed through the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media video of estimated penny-sized hail falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low across northern Arizona and an associated mid level wind maximum across southeast Arizona resulted in high winds, especially downwind of the highest mountains in Cochise County. The most significant damage was to power poles in the Sierra Vista area east of the Huachuca Mountains. A wildfire started by downed power lines near Fort Huachuca was fanned by the winds and spread to over 1800 acres before becoming contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds buffeted eastern Santa Cruz and southwest Cochise counties during the late night hours of October 11 and early morning hours of October 12. Sustained winds over 40 mph and wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph were measured at a number of observing sites for several hours. The highest gust recorded was 70 mph at the Libby Field ASOS at 0017MST. High winds downed or damaged 16 power poles in the Sierra Vista, Fort Huachuca and Hereford areas and other damage was reported to roofs, windows, fences, vehicles and trees. Downed power lines near Fort Huachuca sparked the Site Maverick wildfire that consumed over 1800 acres of grassland before firefighters achieved containment. Power lines were also downed in the Sonoita area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures around 105 degrees were experienced across the Tohono O'odham Nation on September 7. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two males found in the desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 3 to 5 degrees above normal, around 105 degrees, were experienced across the Tohono O'odham Nation causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached on September 7. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two males found in the desert, one on the far western edge of the Tohono O'odham Nation near Sweetwater, and another northeast of Sells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures were experienced west and south of Tucson September 11 through 14. The hottest two days were the 12th and 13th when high temperatures fell just shy of records across much of Pima County and topped out between 104 and 109 degrees. During the four day period, the Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for seven individuals found in the desert, one at Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument, five on the Tohono O'odham Nation, and one in Santa Cruz County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 3 to 5 degrees above normal, around 105 degrees, were experienced across the Tohono O'odham Nation causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached on September 11. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one female found in the desert west of Vamori.

Several trees were blown down, some of which caused additional roof damage. causing roof damage. Some other tree/power line damage in other parts of town. A recreational vehicle was  flipped on its side 5 miles south of Safford. The ASOS at Safford Municipal Airport measured a gust to 68 mph at 1545MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure and an associated mid level wind maximum across southeast Arizona resulted in high winds, especially downwind of the Huachuca Mountains in Cochise County. No significant damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter downwind of the Pinaleno Mountain's Stockton Pass near Highway 191 reported minor tree damage in the area and a peak wind gust of 67 mph at 0339MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near continuous low level moisture and sub freezing temperatures created ideal conditions for rim icing above 8500 feet across the Western Mogollon Rim.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rim ice coated NWR transmission equipment on Mt Elden just northeast of Flagstaff. This caused transition problems to at least one NWR station (Mt Ord near Payson).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough just off the Pacific Northwest coast moved across Arizona with high winds over northern parts of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Window Rock Airport reported sustained winds of 40 MPH and higher between 245 and 255 AM MST. The peak wind gust was 64 MPH at 253 AM. The ADOT wind sensor along I-40 at Lupton measured wind gusts above 58 MPH between 230 and 300 AM.  The peak wind gust was 77 MPH at 233 AM. Power outages from damaged and/or downed power lines were reported in Nazlini, Hunters Point, Ganado Lakes area, and form Tsaile to Wheatfields (for over 6 hours).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough just off the Pacific Northwest coast moved across Arizona with high winds over northern parts of the state.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough just off the Pacific Northwest coast moved across Arizona with high winds over northern parts of the state.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long wave trough moved over Arizona with several short waves that produced snow and lower elevation rain. The snow level started above 7000 feet and then dropped through the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the lower elevations of Yavapai County.  Just over an inch of rain fell in Black Canyon City in 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted southeast Arizona for New Years Eve into New Years Day 2022. This produced in excess of 1 foot of snow across the higher elevations of mountain ranges north of Interstate 10. Wind gusts to 35 mph accompanied the snow at times, but no significant blowing or drifting snow was reported. The strongest winds were farther south and east where gusts of 40 to 50 mph occurred in showers and thunderstorms. Rain was widespread in valley locations with one to two inches common. Some foothills and mid slope areas received over 3 inches of rainfall, but no significant flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow occurred in Ski Valley where 12-18 inches was observed. Slightly lower in elevation at Summerhaven there was 10-12 inches. This event spanned from 2021 into 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following widespread 1 to 2 inches of rain on Christmas Eve, dense fog developed in parts of the Greater Phoenix Area in the morning of Christmas Day, particularly in the southwest and far southeast parts of the metro. No reports of ground traffic being impacted were found. The fog was considered significant seeing as it is a once, maybe twice, a year occurrence in the Phoenix area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chandler Municipal Airport and Phoenix-Mesa Gateway Airport both reported visibilities down to or below a quarter of a mile from 0547MST to 0950MST. There were also multiple photos and videos from this area showing visibilities down to a few hundred feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate to strong atmospheric river aligned over South-central Arizona and led to widespread light to moderate rain across the region. Rain began falling the night of the 23rd and lasted through the evening of the 24th. The Phoenix area measured 1-2 inches and most foothill and mountainous areas measured 2-3 inches, with a few localized spots measuring 3-4 inches. The high rainfall amounts led to minor urban flooding and elevated flows in typically dry washes as well as in several major creeks and rivers. Elevated flows made at least one low water crossing in the Cave Creek area impassable. Flows coming down from Pinal Peak, within the 2021 Telegraph Fire burn area, also made some rural roads in the El Capitan area impassable. Tonto Creek in Tonto Basin experienced a 2-week long period with flow at or above action stage, beginning in the evening of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media photo posted at 1702MST showed a low water crossing along Maddock Road, near 26th Street, was impassable due to flood waters. Timing of impacts estimated from radar. A measured 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rain had fallen in the area by 1700MST. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought locally high winds to the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust was measured at the Kingman Airport (KIGM). Several homes around Kingman had shingle damage to the roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first heat wave of the season pushed temperatures well over 100 degrees in much of the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat Warning criteria were reached four days in a row.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures around 100 degrees were experienced on June 2 on the Tohono O'odham Nation. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two males found in the desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures 5 to 10 degrees above normal, around 100, occurred on the Tohono O'odham Nation causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached on June 2. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two males found in the desert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved southwest across southeast Arizona.  One storm caused extensive tree damage in Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged or uprooted 35 shallow-rooted trees at Evergreen Cemetery in Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures in the upper 90s to 100 degrees were experienced on June 6-7 in the lower elevations of southern Pima County. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for three males found in the desert, one on the Tohono O'odham Nation and 2 in southeast Pima County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures, 5 to 10 degrees above normal, were experienced on June 6-7 with high temperatures in the upper 90s to 100 degrees, causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for three males found in the desert, one on the Tohono O'odham Nation near Vamori, one near Arivaca, and another north of Amado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet July caused the exceptional (D4) drought category and extreme (D3) drought category to cease across nearly all of southeast Arizona, with exceptional drought only remaining in far southeast Cochise County by the end of the month. The extreme (D3) drought category remained across the rest of eastern and central Cochise County, much of Graham and Greenlee counties and parts of the Tohono O'odham Nation. July rainfall varied widely from 2 to 15 inches across southeast Arizona. In areas where the drought lingered through the end of the month this equated to 75 to 125 percent of normal rainfall while areas in which the drought eased further rainfall tallied as much as 400 percent of normal. July ranked as the wettest on record for any month at the Tucson International Airport where 8.06 inches was recorded. The rainfall caused wildfire season to end and soil moisture readings to improve from below the 10th percentile to between the 30th and 70th percentiles by month's end. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir started to rise, but only reached slightly more than 1 percent of capacity by the end of the month.

CZ_NAME: UPPER GILA RIVER AND ARAVAIPA VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human caused Pinnacle Fire started June 10 on Pinnacle Ridge in the Santa Teresa Wilderness of western Graham County. The fire grew to over 15,000 acres within a week in hot, dry and windy conditions and to 34,417 acres by June 25. No fire growth was experienced after June 25, but the fire was not officially contained until July 16. Residents of the Aravaipa, Black Rock and Klondyke communities were evacuated for about two weeks, but no structures were lost. Fire suppression costs tallied $10.5 million.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Horton Fire was the largest of four lightning caused fires that ignited on June 16 north of Hannagan Meadows in the White Mountains in Greenlee County. The four fires, driven by thunderstorm outflow wind gusts in excess of 40 mph on several days, merged into an 11,000 acre fire by June 26 and grew to 12,263 acres by June 29. No fire growth was experienced after this date, but the fire was not officially contained until July 13. Fire suppression costs tallied $3.5 million.

No fire growth was experienced after this date, but the fire was not officially contained until July 13. Fire suppression costs tallied $3.5 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated high based thunderstorms moved west across Graham and Cochise counties. Outflow winds downed several power poles in Graham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph blew across the Gila River Valley. Several power poles were downed in Pima and south of Safford. A wind gust to 54 mph was measured at the Safford Airport ASOS.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptional (D4) and extreme (D3) drought categories continued across a large part of southeast Arizona during June, with the exception of western Pima County.  Most areas only tallied 0.5 or less for the month, which is close to normal. A few locations received 1-1.5 of rain, but that was not enough to make up for record rainfall deficits over the past 12 to 15 months. Soil moisture readings remained below the 5th percentile ranking across most of southeast Arizona except around the 10th percentile ranking in Greenlee and southeast Graham counties. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir remained near zero percent of capacity. Numerous human and lightning caused wild land fires occurred, five of which grew to more than 10,000 acres during the hot, dry and windy month.

CZ_NAME: UPPER GILA RIVER AND ARAVAIPA VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lightning caused Bottom Fire started June 28 in the Gila River Valley on San Carlos Apache tribal lands within Graham County. The fire quickly grew to around 3000 acres by the end of the month due to daily bouts of gusty thunderstorm outflow winds. Nearly daily rounds of thunderstorm winds continued during the first week of July causing the fire to grow to 6004 acres before it was fully contained July 10. Three structures in Calva were temporarily evacuated on July 6 when strong winds caused the fire to break containment lines. Highway 70 was closed for a time and a nearby Eastern Arizona railroad trestle was damaged. Fire suppression costs tallied $1.4 million.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures in the upper 90s to 100 degrees were experienced on June 6-7 in the lower elevations of southern Pima County. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for three males found in the desert, one on the Tohono O'odham Nation and 2 in southeast Pima County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures, 5 to 10 degrees above normal, were experienced on June 6-7 with high temperatures in the upper 90s to 100 degrees, causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for three males found in the desert, one on the Tohono O'odham Nation near Vamori, one near Arivaca, and another north of Amado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Mogollon Rim and moved southwest across Greenlee and Graham counties.  One storm caused lightning and wind damage in Central.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed two large trees, one power pole, a barn and a few small sheds in Central. One of the downed trees smashed through a brick wall. Another of the trees downed two power lines which trapped two people in separate vehicles.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lightning caused Bear Fire started June 16 in the White Mountains in Greenlee County. Thunderstorm outflow wind gusts in excess of 40 mph caused the wildfire to grow to more than 2000 acres on June 20 and resulted in the closure of Highway 191. Rapid fire growth occurred during the next week due to several additional days of gusty thunderstorm outflow winds with 23,480 acres consumed by June 28. Increased humidity inhibited rapid growth thereafter, and during this time of less active fire management the final total reached 24,067 acres by July 8, when Highway 191 was reopened. Fire suppression costs tallied $200,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet July caused the exceptional (D4) drought category and extreme (D3) drought category to cease across nearly all of southeast Arizona, with exceptional drought only remaining in far southeast Cochise County by the end of the month. The extreme (D3) drought category remained across the rest of eastern and central Cochise County, much of Graham and Greenlee counties and parts of the Tohono O'odham Nation. July rainfall varied widely from 2 to 15 inches across southeast Arizona. In areas where the drought lingered through the end of the month this equated to 75 to 125 percent of normal rainfall while areas in which the drought eased further rainfall tallied as much as 400 percent of normal. July ranked as the wettest on record for any month at the Tucson International Airport where 8.06 inches was recorded. The rainfall caused wildfire season to end and soil moisture readings to improve from below the 10th percentile to between the 30th and 70th percentiles by month's end. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir started to rise, but only reached slightly more than 1 percent of capacity by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong monsoon high centered over the Four Corners led to temperatures approaching 110 degrees at times in Phoenix. Temperatures most nights were 3-4 degrees above normal as lows remained in the upper 80s. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 30 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three fatalities (2 direct and 1 indirect) were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level ridge over the Desert Southwest led to continued above normal temperatures along with excessive heat conditions for the middle part of September. Temperatures most afternoons during this time in Phoenix topped out around 108-109 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 10 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time. One of these fatalities included a person who died while hiking in Scottsdale. According to local media reports, a couple from Rhode Island started hiking around noon on the 12th at Brown's Ranch Trailhead in Scottsdale. After splitting up during the hike, the wife never returned to their meeting location. Local authorities responded and discovered that the wife had succumbed to the heat.

A classic bow echo developed along the Mogollon Rim in central Arizona around 1900MST on the 13th and merged with thunderstorm clusters that developed in south-central Arizona around 2030MST to form one southwestward-moving MCS that progressed all the way to the town of Yuma in far southwest Arizona by 0230MST on the 14th. The MCS produced widespread strong winds with localized damaging winds from individual microbursts across the region. The strong winds also generated dense blowing dust in southwest Arizona. In addition to the wind, storms were producing average rain rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour with briefly higher rates in the strongest cells. This led to flash flooding in multiple areas. The town of Gila Bend suffered catastrophic flash flooding after 2 to 4 inches of rain fell over a large portion of the basin containing Sand Tank and Bender Washes, which flow into Gila Bend. A breach of the canal in town due to erosion added to the flash flooding. Significant flow through the washes impacted numerous homes and businesses and there were 2 fatalities. A state of emergency was declared for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following 2 to 4 inches of rain in 1 to 2 hours over the Sand Tank Wash watershed, catastrophic flooding occurred in the small town of Gila Bend with feet of water, mud, and debris moving through town. Storm surveys were completed by NWS Phoenix, Maricopa County Department of Emergency Management (MCDEM), American Red Cross, and Flood Control District of Maricopa County (FCDMC). FCDMC gages on Sand Tank Wash, Bender Wash, and in the Maricopa Mountains measured rainfall amounts around 3 to 4 inches in 1 to 2 hours for an annual exceedance probability of 0.1%. FCDMC found the most significant floodwaters were within Sand Tank Wash, with lesser flows in Bender and Scott Avenue Washes. The NWS Phoenix survey found evidence of out of bank flooding in Sand Tank Wash 1 to 3 miles south of Interstate-8 (I-8) with 2 to 6 feet of water and sizable debris. The streamflow gage on Sand Tank Wash at I-8 reached alarm stage at 0157MST, bank full at 0341MST, and flood stage at 0352MST. The gage crested at 7.69 feet with 5000 cfs of flow at 0408MST. The floodwaters and debris that moved through town caused flood damage to at least 140 homes and 25 businesses. Most structures impacted were between the Gila Bend Canal and Main Street and then along Pima Street. During the flooding, Maricopa County Sheriff's Office rescued at least 30 individuals from their roofs along with others pulled from stranded vehicles. Photos after the water receded showed the canal was eroded and breached where Sand Tank Wash flows over it. Photos also showed 2 to 5 foot water lines both on the outside and inside of many of the structures, piles of debris, damaged property and vehicles, and erosion. Of the homes MCDEM assessed, 21 were deemed uninhabitable after the flooding. Unfortunately there were 2 direct fatalities with this event. One made a heroic effort to save a stranded motorist before they themselves were swept away in their own vehicle. The other was swept away in one of the flooded washes. Additionally, many farm animals were killed. Besides the fatalities, no other injuries were reported. A state of emergency was declared for this event.

Upper level ridging over much of the western United States led to deadly heat at the beginning of July. Temperatures at Phoenix Sky Harbor during this time peaked at 107 degrees, which is normal for this time of year. However, low temperatures only cooled to around 90 degrees at times, providing little overnight relief. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 14 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three fatalities (1 direct and 2 indirect) were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure centered over the Great Basin resulted in temperatures climbing above normal during the first part of September. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 15 heat-associated deaths occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One indirect fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge strengthened over the Desert Southwest during the middle part of June with the center of high pressure becoming focused over the Four Corners region. As a result, temperatures warmed up well above normal reaching excessive heat conditions. High temperatures climbed at or above 115 degrees each day (6 days) between the 15th-20th, breaking Phoenix's record for consecutive days of 115+ degree temperatures. Temperatures in Phoenix peaked on the 17th at 118 degrees, breaking the record for the date and becoming the hottest temperature for the whole year. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, sixty heat-associated fatalities occurred as a result of this heat wave.

However, low temperatures only cooled to around 90 degrees at times, providing little overnight relief. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 14 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three indirect fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong monsoon high centered over the Four Corners led to temperatures approaching 110 degrees at times in Phoenix. Temperatures most nights were 3-4 degrees above normal as lows remained in the upper 80s. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 30 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four indirect fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure encompassing the Desert Southwest led to above normal temperatures, with excessive heat conditions occurring across the Phoenix area between the 9th and 11th. At Phoenix Sky Harbor, temperatures climbed above 110 degrees for six consecutive days between the 6th and 11th, with the warmest highs peaking at 112 degrees. Overnight low temperatures offered little relief from the heat as temperatures only fell to around 90 degrees most nights. On the 9th, Phoenix recorded a low temperature of 93 degrees, setting a new record warm low for the date. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 49 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two direct fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge strengthened over the Desert Southwest during the middle part of June with the center of high pressure becoming focused over the Four Corners region. As a result, temperatures warmed up well above normal reaching excessive heat conditions. High temperatures climbed at or above 115 degrees each day (6 days) between the 15th-20th, breaking Phoenix's record for consecutive days of 115+ degree temperatures. Temperatures in Phoenix peaked on the 17th at 118 degrees, breaking the record for the date and becoming the hottest temperature for the whole year. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, sixty heat-associated fatalities occurred as a result of this heat wave.

A broad upper level ridge over the Desert Southwest led to continued above normal temperatures along with excessive heat conditions for the middle part of September. Temperatures most afternoons during this time in Phoenix topped out around 108-109 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 10 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time. One of these fatalities included a person who died while hiking in Scottsdale. According to local media reports, a couple from Rhode Island started hiking around noon on the 12th at Brown's Ranch Trailhead in Scottsdale. After splitting up during the hike, the wife never returned to their meeting location. Local authorities responded and discovered that the wife had succumbed to the heat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge situated over much of the western United States at the end of July led to heat that resulted in several fatalities. One of these fatalities included a woman visiting from Boston who died after becoming overheated while hiking on Camelback Mountain. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, a total of 7 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 31-year-old woman visiting from Boston went hiking with a man on Echo Canyon Trail on Camelback Mountain around 1PM MST. The woman became overheated about halfway up the trail and decided to turn around and go back down, while the man continued up the trail. The woman was later discovered and pronounced dead near a home next to the mountain after she was believed to be seeking help. The man and woman were reportedly hiking without any water.

A wet July caused the exceptional (D4) drought category and extreme (D3) drought category to cease across nearly all of southeast Arizona, with exceptional drought only remaining in far southeast Cochise County by the end of the month. The extreme (D3) drought category remained across the rest of eastern and central Cochise County, much of Graham and Greenlee counties and parts of the Tohono O'odham Nation. July rainfall varied widely from 2 to 15 inches across southeast Arizona. In areas where the drought lingered through the end of the month this equated to 75 to 125 percent of normal rainfall while areas in which the drought eased further rainfall tallied as much as 400 percent of normal. July ranked as the wettest on record for any month at the Tucson International Airport where 8.06 inches was recorded. The rainfall caused wildfire season to end and soil moisture readings to improve from below the 10th percentile to between the 30th and 70th percentiles by month's end. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir started to rise, but only reached slightly more than 1 percent of capacity by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet July caused the exceptional (D4) drought category and extreme (D3) drought category to cease across nearly all of southeast Arizona, with exceptional drought only remaining in far southeast Cochise County by the end of the month. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving across the state promoted the development of convection over portions of Maricopa County during the afternoon on the 15th. With anomalously high amounts of moisture in place along with high instability, thunderstorms were capable of producing strong winds and locally heavy rainfall, which led to some reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Surprise emergency manager reported 20 trees down in multiple locations along Sunrise Boulevard between Remington Drive and Mountain View Boulevard. Radar estimated winds were between 40-50 mph at the time, which is consistent with a 48 mph gust measured by a mesonet station, MAMD, at 1.9 miles W of Beardsley. No injuries reported.
